title: Introducing Odyssey-2: A General-Purpose World Model
text:
Introducing Odyssey-2: A General-Purpose World Model
A frontier world model
that
brings video to life
Oliver Cameron
October 27th, 2025
A new form of audio-visual intelligence
Today, every video is a pre-recorded, fixed capture that plays out the same way for everyone. At Odyssey, we asked ourselves the question: what if video could feel alive? What if it could be generated and interacted with instantly? What if video shifted from something passive to something interactive?
With
Odyssey-2
, weâve taken a major step forward on that journey. Odyssey-2 is a frontier world model that dreams AI video instantly that you can interact with. You experience it much like a language model: you type, and the video responds in the moment. It feels like magic.
Interactive videos, all streamed in real time
World models are nascent, and so is Odyssey-2, but we hope that when you experience this model you see the same exciting potential we do. In secondsâand with a few words typedâOdyssey-2 begins streaming minutes of imagined video to any screen or device. This feels like the beginning of a fundamental shift for AI, media, and much more.
Experience Odyssey-2
Experience Odyssey-2
AI-imagined video you can interact with
Unlocking open-ended interactivity in video models
Foundational to Odyssey-2 is a video model with deep, general knowledge of the world. Like most video models, it begins life relying on both the future and the past when generating. This works well for clips with fixed endings, but prevents the kind of open-ended interactivity needed to make video feel alive. In a truly interactive stream, any action at any moment alters all possible futures.
Type a prompt and the model evolves the video
To make a video model interactive, it must generate the future without knowing it in advance, responding only to whatâs happened so far. In other words, it must be causal and autoregressive, generating each frame solely from the context of prior frames and your actions. With Odyssey-2, weâve made this possible through a novel multi-stage training pipeline that transitions the model to causal behaviorâyielding a real-time, action-aware video model that responds continuously to input. As the video plays, you shape it in real time using natural text promptsâmuch like talking to a language model. The result is a continuous stream of multi-minute video that listens, adapts, and reacts.
Real-time AI video shaped by open-ended text or audio is a topic of research we're excited to explore deeper, while continuing to explore the navigation conditioning and long-term memory we focused on with
Odyssey-1
âa research preview we released earlier this year.
Odyssey-2 is really, really fast
Video only feels alive when itâs generated instantly. Most bidirectional video models today take 1-2 minutes to generate only 5 seconds of footage. Odyssey-2 on the other hand begins streaming video instantlyâproducing a new frame of video every 50 milliseconds (meaning imagined video is streamed at 20 frames per second).
Odyssey-2 begins streaming video immediately
The latency reduction is game-changing, and changes whatâs possible with AI video. Instead of waiting minutes for a short clip to be generated, you instead interact with a stream of video in the moment. Achieving this while retaining the generality and fidelity of the model required deep optimization at every level. We tuned our model architecture, data pipeline, and inference stack to balance speed, quality, and responsivenessâso Odyssey-2 can dream freely, without delay.
Every frame generated in less than 50ms
World models are burgeoning world simulators
World models predict the next frame using only the past, and then roll forward. Long rollouts punish bad guesses, so the model must internalize the dynamics of how motion, lighting, and contact evolve over time. In doing so, they become implicit world simulatorsâsystems that learn to model and generate the world in real time.
Waves on the ocean are a great example. From prior video frames of a wave, you can infer surface slope, curvature, and a velocity field. With that, you can predict what comes next: the crest advances, troughs fill, foam drifts, highlights slide, and the wave bends around a rock. Although early on this journey, thatâs exactly what Odyssey-2 does, all learned from decades of video data. This same concept applies generally to dynamics and behaviors of many types.
The model learns complex physics and dynamics
Language models showed how far a simple next-step objective can goâpredicting the next word unlocked reasoning and creativity. models take that idea further. By learning to predict the next frame of video, they begin to approximate the rules that govern our world. As these models learn to act and react to the highest levels of realism, they will transition from enabling emergent media to a general-purpose world simulator.
From fixed media to emergent media
Video is fast shifting from passive to interactive. As that shift takes hold, new consumer and enterprise applications (our API is coming soon!) will emerge across gaming, film, education, social media, advertising, training, companionship, simulation, and much more.
Entirely new applications will be unlocked
Imagine stepping into an old photo and exploring that memory once more. Taking a guided tour of an ancient civilization as if you were there. Learning piano with instant feedback. Asking Einstein to teach you physicsâasking him questions along the way. Learning a new language by speaking with people on the streets of an imagined Paris or Tokyo. Entering a sports game at a pivotal moment and changing the outcome. These applications aren't solved today, but
Odyssey-2
is an early, humble step towards this exciting future.
Experience Odyssey-2
Experience Odyssey-2
AI-imagined video you can interact with
The incredible Odyssey team
The team that brought this to life
Odyssey-2 was made possible by the incredible Odyssey team: Andy Kolkhorst, Aravind Kaimal, Ben Graham, Boyu Liu, Derek Sarshad, Fabian GÃ¼ra, James Grieve, Jeff Hawke, Jenny Seidenschwarz, Jessica Inman, Jon Sadeghi, Kristy McDonough, Nima Rezaeian, Oliver Cameron, Philip Petrakian, Richard Shen, Robin Tweedie, Sarah King, Sirish Srinivasan, Vinh-Dieu Lam, and Zygmunt Åenyk.
